text: The biochemical adaptation (cytochrome c concentration) in skeletal muscle follows a first-order pattern with increasing daily exercise duration, meaning gains diminish as duration increases beyond a threshold.

As exercise intensity increases, the daily duration required to achieve peak biochemical adaptation decreases, suggesting higher-intensity sessions can be shorter while still maximizing mitochondrial adaptation.
Slow-twitch and fast-twitch red muscle fibers reach maximal mitochondrial adaptation (cytochrome c) at submaximal exercise intensities, with no further gains at higher intensities.
specific_value: 19 to 29 nmol/g cytochrome c, plateau below 80% VO2max

text: Fast-twitch white (glycolytic) muscle fibers only begin adapting at approximately 80% VO2max intensity, with adaptation increasing exponentially as intensity rises further.
specific_value: ~80% VO2max threshold, 200% increase at highest intensity

text: Different muscle fiber types have fundamentally different intensity-adaptation relationships, likely driven by the ordered recruitment pattern of motor units from slow-twitch to fast-twitch fibers.

Low-to-moderate intensity running is sufficient to maximize mitochondrial enzyme adaptation in oxidative (slow-twitch and fast-twitch red) muscle fibers, supporting the value of easy aerobic running for endurance development.
